local_names.romanized_local: Xiāo Huà Bǐng Gān (Chinese); Biskut Digestif (Malay); Ṭaijasṭiv Piskaṭ (Tamil)

aliases: 消化饼干 (Xiāo Huà Bǐng Gān)

chinese: 消化饼干 (Xiāo Huà Bǐng Gān)

region_of_origin: United Kingdom (manufactured and sold in Singapore by pladis / McVitie's)
food_subgroup: Semi-sweet wholemeal biscuit; made with wholemeal wheat flour, oat flour, vegetable oil (palm oil), and sugar; baked

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for digestive biscuits. Closest parent is grain-based biscuit/cracker (FOODON:03301710 — cracker). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Grain milling products — biscuits and crackers (FoodEx2 level-2 group GR; closest defensible parent for a wholemeal wheat biscuit). No exact FoodEx2 leaf code exists for digestive biscuit; closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a leaf-level code for McVitie's digestive biscuit. Group-level code GR (grain milling products) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for digestive biscuit; null assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 biscuits (approximately 30 g), providing approximately 138 kcal, 19.5 g carbohydrate, 5.5 g fat, and 1.8 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: McVitie's Digestive Biscuits — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Crackers, whole-wheat) cross-referenced with McVitie's Singapore product label (pladis Singapore)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy; McVitie's Singapore product label (pladis Singapore)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole-wheat crackers/biscuits used as primary nutrient anchor, cross-referenced and adjusted against McVitie's Singapore product label (pladis Singapore) for energy, fat, saturated fat, sodium, and sugar values. Where label values differed from USDA, label values were preferred for branded product accuracy. Micronutrient values (B vitamins, minerals) sourced from USDA SR Legacy for wholemeal wheat biscuit type.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of McVitie's Digestive Biscuit — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Foundation)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 59 (medium) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for McVitie's Digestive Biscuit. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (59 × 19.5) / 100 ≈ 11.5 per 30 g serving (2 biscuits).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 300 mg/100 g (90 mg per 30 g serving); saturated fat 8.3 g/100 g from palm oil
hypertension.guidance: At the standard 2-biscuit (30 g) serving, sodium intake is 90 mg — acceptable within a hypertension diet — but patients who consume 4–6 biscuits (60–90 g) in a single sitting will ingest 180–270 mg sodium per snack occasion, and the palm-oil-derived saturated fat may contribute to LDL elevation with habitual high intake; portion control is essential.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 59 (medium), glycemic load 11.5 per 30 g serving; 19.5 g available carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: McVitie's Digestive Biscuit has a medium GI of 59 and a glycemic load of approximately 11.5 per 2-biscuit serving — a manageable glycemic impact in isolation, but the product is frequently misidentified by Singapore T2DM patients as a 'safe' low-GI snack; patients should be counselled that consuming 4–6 biscuits triples the glycemic load and that lower-GL alternatives such as a small handful of unsalted nuts provide superior satiety with negligible glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.3 g/100 g (2.5 g per 30 g serving) from palm oil; trans fat confirmed 0 g; dietary fibre 4.7 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 30 g serving contributes approximately 2.5 g saturated fat (primarily palmitic acid from palm oil), which is modest at the standard serving size but accumulates rapidly with habitual multi-biscuit consumption; the wholemeal wheat and oat flour provide 1.4 g dietary fibre per serving, offering a minor cardiovascular benefit, and the confirmed absence of trans fat is a positive attribute for a commercially baked product.

llm_training.natural_language_description: McVitie's Digestive Biscuit (消化饼干; Biskut Digestif) is the most widely consumed biscuit in Singapore — a semi-sweet, dense, slightly crumbly disc approximately 7 cm in diameter with a characteristic grainy, mildly nutty texture derived from wholemeal wheat flour and oat flour. It is baked with vegetable oil (palm oil), sugar, sodium bicarbonate, and salt. The name 'digestive' is a major source of patient misconception in Singapore: it does not mean low-calorie, low-GI, or specifically beneficial for gut health. The name originated from an 1892 marketing claim by McVitie and Price that the sodium bicarbonate content aided digestion — a claim that was never clinically validated. Despite this, Singapore patients with Type 2 Diabetes frequently consider digestive biscuits a 'safe' snack choice. The clinical reality: GI ~59 (medium), 138 kcal per 2-biscuit (30 g) serving, 19.5 g available carbohydrate, glycemic load ~11.5. The digestive biscuit does hold a meaningful GI advantage over rice crackers (GI ~80) and cream crackers (GI ~78) due to the wholemeal wheat and oat flour providing modest dietary fibre (1.4 g per 30 g serving). Among biscuits available in Singapore, digestive biscuits rank as one of the better choices — but they should not be confused with a truly healthy snack. McVitie's is the dominant brand in Singapore, sold at NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, 7-Eleven, Guardian, and Watsons. Standard Singapore packet = 400 g (~23 biscuits). Nutritionally per 100 g: 460 kcal, 6 g protein, 18.3 g fat (8.3 g saturated), 65 g carbohydrate, 19 g sugar, 4.7 g dietary fibre, 300 mg sodium. Trans fat is confirmed zero. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (no animal-derived fat in formulation). Suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets. Contains gluten (wheat and oats). Dairy-free.
meal_context: afternoon snack with Milo or tea
T2DM patient snack (frequently misidentified as safe)

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole-wheat biscuit/cracker type) cross-referenced with McVitie's Singapore product label (pladis Singapore). GI value of 59 sourced directly from the University of Sydney GI Database for McVitie's Digestive Biscuit (exact match). All values are consistent across sources. Confidence is high.
notes: trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per McVitie's Singapore product label (pladis Singapore) — product formulation explicitly states 0 g trans fat. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero: product contains no animal-derived fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for wholemeal wheat biscuit type. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg are confirmed zero: no animal-derived ingredients and no fortification declared on McVitie's Singapore label; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for this biscuit type. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported on McVitie's Singapore product label and not available in USDA SR Legacy for this specific product formulation. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported on McVitie's Singapore product label and not available in USDA SR Legacy for this specific biscuit type. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 59 × available carbohydrate 19.5 g per 30 g serving) / 100 = 11.5. The 'digestive' name does not confer any clinically validated digestive health benefit; it is a historical marketing term from 1892.
